sentences.text: We are--
--already in Auschwitz.
We came into Auschwitz.

Let me tell you, besides the shock that you live without any blanket, without anything, eight people on a cot, one near the other-- and if you had to go to the toilet, you would be scared that this guy is going to catch you and kill you-- so I was lucky that I was not in the front of the barrack.
But where he lived, he had his little cubicle there.
But I was in the back of the barrack.
And I remember they gave us, they took us out to appell, where you take mutze up and mutze down, mutze off and mutze on.
You take your cap on quite a few times.
And you align and you have to stay in the line.
And this SS man goes by and checks and counts to make sure that everybody is there.
And they-- we were five in a line.
And they gave to the first one in the line-- to each first one in the line, they gave a bowl of soup, a bowl of soup which you couldn't drink.
It was quite-- quite soup, with potatoes.
There was some-- some things in that soup.
And no spoon.
And everybody had to go and try to-- for the first four days that I was in Auschwitz, I didn't eat any soup except the bread.
And I must tell you the shock when I saw the bread.
It looked like a brick.
It tasted like a brick.
It was heavy like a brick.
It looked-- I don't know.
It wasn't-- I don't know what it was made of.
It wasn't made of flour, not the kind of bread we were used in Sighet.
At least there we had good bread.
It was terrible.
So they gave out the bread.
That's what I lived on for four days.
On the fourth day, I remember we were standing in line to be tattooed.
And then, before they reached us, just before they reached our line, our-- our-- my line, the-- the-- an SS man came and said, halt.
He needs people for work.
So they took us.
From here on, everybody turn around and walk to the train.
We went with the train for like five or six hours.
No, not at Auschwitz.
I don't remember the barrack number.
All the barracks looked the same.
All the people were treated the same.
I don't remember much of those four days, except that-- that I was shocked to hear that they don't give you toilet paper.
How do you go to the bathroom without toilet paper?
But then if you don't eat for four days, you don't need the bathroom.
You don't need the bathroom.
You don't go so much to the bathroom.
So it wasn't as bad as I was frightened.
But I remember the shock when he said, if somebody ask me for toilet paper, I kill him on the spot.
Obviously, people were coming.
I mean, the first thing you want, you want to go someplace.
No toilet paper.
Absolutely nothing.
Who were you with in the barracks?
I was-- most of the people were our people from our transport, most of them.
Very few were not from our transport.
So because they took from that, when we were standing in line, they took us together.
And then we were transported to work also together.
So we were like heimisch, like heimische people.
There was one of my neighbors there.
He was a very great organizer.
He took care of me, and he saved my life tens of times, tens of times, endangered my life by doing that.

Well, he would-- in Auschwitz, when we were going out of Auschwitz, suddenly in the line before we embark the train, he had-- he gives me a-- a half a blanket and tells me to put it under my shirt, to wrap myself in.
And I was afraid.
I was afraid if they catch us with that piece of blanket, they kill you, because what they did, probably, somebody tore a blanket in three and four, and took-- everybody got a piece of blanket.
He was such an organizer.
From where did he take a piece of blanket?
I can't imagine.
And I don't know to this day.
I never, never went-- came-- went around to it to ask him.
But I sure enough had the piece of blanket.
When we arrived in the train, we were sitting down.
I had a piece of blanket with me, which kept me warm, because it was cold.
Even if it was May, it was still cold.
We were-- we were going in the train for like five, six hours.
And I remember I needed-- I needed to pee.
And they don't let you.
And there is no pile.
They don't let you do it.
I had a bad time.
I know that now when I go into a place, the first thing is to look for a place where if I need to go to the bathroom that there is a men's room there.
And I'm uncomfortable when I don't see it, because such an impression it had on me, that we couldn't go out.
I thought I'm going to burst open.
But we arrived after the six hours--
Not really.
Not really.
In Auschwitz, we didn't do any work.
Nothing.
We were kind of separated.
We were kind of isolated, until they took us to-- took us to the train to take us to Wolfsberg.
No.
We didn't see anybody.
We-- we only saw-- when we walked out of the bath barrack, of that barrack that held the bath, we walked out, we saw women.
A little bit further, we saw women all sheared their hair off.
And on every woman, I thought, this is this sister, this is the other sister.
You couldn't really-- I mean, they were dressed also in these pajamas.
You couldn't recognize them without their hair.
But everybody was imagining, this is my sister, this is my sister, the other is my mother.
You would imagine.
But my mother, I understand, wasn't even there.
And my younger sister wasn't even there.
She didn't even reach the bath, because they took them to-- straight to the crematorium.
No.
Just-- just once-- once one of the guys asked the kapo-- when we were standing in line, the kapos were watching us.
He asked the kapo, is there a way out of here?
He said, you see there?
He showed the smoke.
He showed the chimney.
He said, yeah, there is.
Through there, there is a way out of here.
But there was a way out.
They took us out from Auschwitz.
They took us out by train.
They took us.
And they transported us to-- about five, six-- after five, six hours train, and we came to a place called Wolfsberg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, this was in Niederschlesien in lower Silesia.
It was about 38 kilometers, which you later found out-- about 38 kilometers from Breslau, a town called Breslau.
I don't know what they call it today.
I think Wroclaw they call it today in Polish somebody told me.
But I'm not sure about that.
I thought all the time that Breslau and Bratislava is the same thing.
But somebody told me it's not.
So he said it's called Wroclaw.
And he seems to know.
The camp was called Wolfsberg.
Now, the first six weeks in that camp, we were building the camp.
There was absolutely nothing.
We were building those-- they call them Finnish barracks, round tents, not made of-- of cloth, but made of dicht.
That is with plywood, kind of plywood.
They were round, and inside there was a wooden circle.
And-- and there was some-- how do you say, kash?-- how do you say?-- straw.
And on that straw, we would lie down.
That's where we would sleep.
Now, for six weeks, we didn't have any facilities to wash our clothes.
There was a small creek running through the camp.
In that creek, we would wash in the morning without soap, just wash ourselves.
And in the evenings after work, we would wash our shirts sometimes, and put it on immediately.
You couldn't-- it had to dry on you, because if you put it out to dry, they would steal it.
Somebody would take it away.
You wouldn't-- this was our only shirt that you have.
This was your only-- only clothes that you have, your shirt and your pants and your shoes.
The inmates were people from our town, most of them-- most of them.
And there were like 500 or 600 Salonicans.
Now, the Salonican Jews, they were-- for some reason, they were-- they were stronger people.
They were-- they were-- most of them were working in the harbor, you know taking off, loading off loads from ships, and et cetera, et cetera.
This was their professions.
And they were-- by nature, they were strong.
They were people who worked physical work, so they were stronger people.
And immediately they took over the-- the government to govern the camp.
They would-- they would be the ones who were over the latrines.
Latrines were the toilets, you know, open latrines, the huge place where you-- 20 people would sit down in one shot-- sit.
You wouldn't sit.
You would stand, you just-- and do what you had to do.
And they would-- they would clean those latrines.
But because of that, they wouldn't go out to work.
They would work in the camps.
So they had access to food.
They had access to things.
They had the better place to live.
You know, they were kind of dominating the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The camp-- the tents, these Finnish tents were-- we had like, I think, 12 blocks.
A line up the hill, like seven or eight or nine tents like this in a line, it was called one block.
And each block had a blockalteste who was the-- the overseer of the block, the responsible for the block, who was an inmate, a Jew.
These were mostly Polish people who were older in the camp already.
They were-- they were already-- their sufferings went on for like four years or so.
So they hated us, because when they were in trouble, they said, we still had-- had a good life in Hungaria, in Hungary, or wherever we were.
So they hated us.
They wanted to get even with us, so they tried to-- to-- you know, a slave, when he becomes a king, you know what he does to his people.
He's worse than-- than a normal king who comes from a-- from royal blood, from royal lineage.
What sorts of things were they--
So that's how they behave.
Well, they would beat you up for everything, for every smallest thing.
If you weren't running fast enough to come in line to the appell, which every morning you would get up-- they would wake you up like 3 o'clock in the morning.
The appell was only like 6 o'clock.
About 4 o'clock, you would get a liquid called coffee, which was coffee ersatz.
It was a kind of substitute to coffee.
The Germans didn't have coffee, not for us, definitely not for us.
But even for the population later they didn't have coffee.
So they were eating their-- they were drinking the ersatz coffee.
So we'd get-- we'd get a little water, hot water.
It was very good in the morning to warm you up.
And then you would stay in line 'til 6 o'clock.
At 6 o'clock, the-- the commander of the-- of the SS would come out and count everybody and everybody.
Every blockalteste would give a report, how many people he has, how many people he is sending out to work.
And they would count you when you come back.

And if there was a miscount-- and there always was a miscount, so they would count three times and four times and five-- you would spend an hour-- an hour counting in the morning and more than an hour in the evening while you were dog-tired after a very hard day's work, because we worked.
What I worked, I worked first at a certain firma that was called HUTA, HUTA, H-U-T-T-A, a German firm.
And they were-- they were cutting trees, cleaning trees from the forest.
So we were cutting these huge trees.
A brother of my very close friend of mine was killed by a tree.
A tree fell and he ran the wrong side, and he got killed by a tree in that camp, at the beginning in Wolfsberg.
And so you would have to be very careful, you know.
They taught you how to cut the trees.
And then you would peel off the trees.
And then you would send the trees down the mountain, you know, those logs, those huge ones.
And then I worked later for a firma called Dubiner who would build houses, houses for the Italian prisoners.
When Badoglio took over Italy and Mussolini was ran away, so the Italians were not working with the Germans hand in hand.
So they took their armament and they kept them as prisoners of war.
So we build their houses.
And then, Later, I worked at the small firm called Firma Angrick.
And we were only three Jewish boys and there were two-- two Germans.
And they taught us how to make the central heating for those buildings.
And these work there was pretty good.
The whole-- the life in Wolfsberg for the nine months that we were there was considerably good, meaning by good was that you had for three men a bread.
Later a little, you got four men a bread.
And at the end, you got 18 men a bread, the same loaf of bread divided into 18, so you got a cubicle of bread.
But at the beginning, we got three men a bread.
That was-- for me, it was plenty.
I was never a big eater.
And we got the coffee in the morning.
And then we got, about 12 o'clock, you got a good soup, a good soup, potatoes and [INAUDIBLE] and some cabbage soup.
At the beginning, I had a cousin with me.
He would eat half of my soup because I couldn't finish the whole bowl of soup at lunch at work.
Later, you got so hungry that you didn't have enough.
They gave you only-- in the evening, we came back, they gave us another soup.
Later, they give you only one soup.
And you came back at work, you got nothing.
So you worked the whole day with one soup.
And you divided the bread for 18.
It was real terrible.
You were so hungry that you can't imagine.
But this was really only, I would say, the last months of being in Wolfsberg.
Relatively, it was good.
We had a bath.
Every-- every week we could take a hot bath.
We were-- our clothes were de-loused.
I started telling you at the beginning, we didn't have facilities.
We had lice that if you put down your cap, it would walk.
It would move away, so many lice were there.
The lice would dig into your flesh.
They were white lice.
You would-- you would dig them out, and you were wounded, all your body was.
When you went to wash yourself, you had all those signs of the lice where you dug them out, and blood was running from the-- from those wounds.
There was great danger there of typhoid.
I can't imagine how typhoid didn't break out in that camp.
But after six week, we got a bath, and then lousing facilities, so do it and louse us.
And it was pretty well.
This was 'til winter of 1945.
Well, we knew, more or less, because we came-- we came-- it was-- it was Shavuot.
It was a few weeks before Shavuot, which was the day the Torah was given, Pentecost is what you would call.
A few weeks, about two or three weeks before.
So we knew Shavuot, and from there on, we kept the counting, so we knew.
We knew.
And somehow, news travelled, because I used to go from one camp to the other about 5 miles away to Wustegiersdorf to bring planks, because we were building barracks for the wintertime.
Winter, if we would-- would have lived in the-- in those tents, in those Finnish tents, we would've been frozen.
So we lived in-- we built barracks and we lived in the barracks.
And some of the escorts were old people.
They were wearing SS insignias, but they were not SS people.
They forced them to wear those uniforms.
They were just-- they were just army people, you know, people that should have retired.
But they took the old people to-- to guard us.
That were only very few SS people around those camps.
The others were just in the uniform, older people.
And they would talk to us.
I remember at work, at this Firma Angrick, there was one very old man.
He was-- must have been over 70.
And he was one foreman to teach us how to make this installation, the central installations to-- for those houses for the Italian prisoners.
And he used to bring me sometimes a little bread.
And he would say, Izchak-- Izak, he would call me-- I put a little bread there.
He had to hide it, so nobody sees, because if somebody sees and tells about him, he would be in trouble.
So-- and he would talk to me politics.
He would say, Izak, do you know that in 1933, Jewish people financed Hitler?
He was a Communist, obviously.
He was a working man.
Do you know that they-- the Jewish people helped Hitler to come to power?
They financed him?
Which, later, we found out was true.
So they used to tell us-- these people used tell us news, used to tell us what's happening in the world.
They used to tell us all kinds of things that-- What kinds of news were they--
Little bits, here and there.
Well, if something great was happening, they would tell us.
If something-- they wouldn't-- they wouldn't give us newspapers.

But we got sometimes a newspaper from the Italians in Italian, sometimes a piece of paper from the German-- a German paper that the Italians would cut out because they would also have a censored paper.
And very few people you would find that could read the paper, although, we all understood even then-- now I speak Italian fluently and read and write.
But even then, I remember that an Italian gave me a piece of paper, and I could understand a lot of words, because Romanian and Italian are very, very closely-related languages.
So the writing is exactly the same.
So I would understand many words from those articles.
So news was coming.
News was coming around, was going around.
And then we started walking when we heard the cannons in Breslau.
They [BACKGROUND NOISE] took us-- want to stop?
No.
They took us on a march.
They took the children away.
They took some trucks.
The young children, they took away.
And they say they are taking them to a hospital.
But we know where they took them.
They took them to gas them.
And we were going-- we were going on a march for four days.
We walked.
There was snow.
The cars, the camions who brought them the food couldn't reach us because they wouldn't-- they didn't-- didn't have enough power to climb those-- those-- those hills.
And we were for four days without out any food at all, without any food.
We would eat snow.
We would-- we would just eat what you had with you when you walked out, which wasn't much, couldn't have been much.
And we were in a place called Schonbrunn.
We were like 2,000 people, the whole camp, I think, almost the whole camp, except the children.
There were like 20 children that they took away, because even the children were hiding and they wouldn't want to go, because they knew where they are going to take them.
So we were about 2,000 people.
They put us into two barracks, two huge barracks.
And we were there for another two days without food.
W were like six days without real food.
And then, the sixth day, I remember I was so weak that I laid down on the snow.
And I said, I give up.
I can't take it anymore.
I can't live on snow anymore.
And I had what is called a camp father.
I had a camp brother, the one who I said saved my life so many times.
And we had-- we had one olderly man who we arranged a job, a very good job.
He was heating-- those buildings that we built, he was heating them.
Now, this camp brother of mine, he used to organize potatoes.
So we didn't eat the soup that they gave us at work.
The old man, our camp father, he would make us soup.
It would taste like heaven, a good soup.
We ate it warm in-- in his place where he had that little room where the ovens were to heat the central heating, the central heating of that huge house that we built.
So he laid upon me, and blew air, resuscitated me.
And he talked to me.
He warmed me up with his body.
And he said, Izchakam, you will see.
Be strong.
You will see.
The food will arrive soon.
You will see.
He was still upon me that somebody yelled out that the first car, the first truck is arriving with the provision, with the food for us.
And really, a few minutes later, they were all already giving us-- there was this honey, frozen honey.
And they cut it into pieces.
And you could get a little honey and lick that honey.
That was-- that was already life.
You know, that meant life.
And this is how I survived those six days.
After that, they-- we didn't march more than about one day to a train.
And they put us again on an open car, open train.
And it was winter.
And we went through Czechoslovakia.
I remember that on the day of Purim-- on the day of our Purim, we were standing for the whole day aside from the Skoda factory in Czechoslovakia.
Skoda was the biggest factory.
They were making cars, the Skoda cars.
They have today also Skoda cars.
They were making armament for-- at that time, they converted it to make armament for the Germans.
And every-- every Czechoslovakian worker who came out to have his lunch threw the lunch over the fence to our-- into our wagons, because the wagons were open.
They threw theirs in.
And then, the train was very long.

And part-- one of the-- one wagon or two of the train were standing under a bridge, where the passengers from other trains, the civilian passenger would go, come and go, to the train and from the train.
And all the women, we saw it, were pulling out whatever they had, food, in their satchels or in their bags, handbags.
They would pour it out into the trains.
So I remember the Purim there was-- we called it the nes of Purim, the miracle of Purim.
That day, we were standing the whole day.
And then, next day, we started moving.
We went for three days.
Every day, we got-- like 10 people got a loaf of bread.
They would throw in-- we had so many people.
They wouldn't know how many.
Like we were 60-- 60 people.
They would throw in six loaves of bread, the SS men, because in front of every wagon, there was a kind of cubicle where an SS man sat.
And he could watch that nobody climbs out from the wagon, because the wagons were open, so maybe somebody wants to jump out.
So he could see.
They would watch out.
In front and in back of the-- of every wagon, there would be an SS man with a rifle.
So they would throw in three breads from this side, three breads from this side.
And we would divide those breads, 10-- Did people fight over the bread?
No.
No.
No.
No, because you knew that this is the bread that you get.
But you did fight if some people froze.
And there were people who froze to dead-- to death.
So those people's portion, I mean, you would give everybody a piece of bread and he would freeze after that.
He didn't have time to eat it.
Over those portions, they would fight.
I remember one day, I think it was the third day before we arrived to Ebensee, they gave us-- they threw in some cans of-- of meat.
And the group I was in, to divide that can of meat, we got a can that was blown up.
It was almost burst open.
That means that the can-- the-- the contents were not good.
The water, or the soup, whatever it was in the meat was green.
This, if you ate it today, you would probably die the next day from that.
And I can't forget how well it tastes, how good it tasted.
You can't imagine.
We were so hungry that even that food that gone bad, that meat, that little meat gone bad, we ate it.
And we-- we licked our fingers after that, so-- so good did it taste.
